context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i'm stuck on PS3 rn, esp Problem 1(c) with the decreasing derived variable. i think it’s something w/ base-\(n\), but idk how to make the proof work.
exchanges.tutor: Good start noticing the hint! Let's build on it. If you treat the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n-1}) as the digits of a base-n number, what specific number does that give you? And what happens to that number when you make a good reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the hint (base-n) but hasn't yet articulated what the derived variable actually is. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to think concretely about what a base-n interpretation of the permutation gives them, and why that might be useful. I'll keep it short and Socratic, prompting them to construct the variable themselves.

exchanges.student: i think it’d be \(a_0n^{n-1}+a_1n^{n-2}+\cdots+a_{n-1}\). my guess is a good reversal makes it smaller, but i’m not fully seeing why fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's the right variable. Now focus on just the two endpoints of the reversal: positions i and j, where a_i > a_j (since it's a good reversal). How do the contributions of those two positions to the base-n number change after the reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the derived variable. Now I need to guide them to understand why a good reversal decreases it. Rather than explaining, I should ask them to think about what a good reversal does to a specific inverted pair (i,j) where a_i > a_j, and how swapping those values (plus reversing everything in between) affects the base-n number. A concrete small example might help them see the key insight.

exchanges.student: before, those parts are \(a_i n^{n-1-i}+a_j n^{n-1-j}\), and after they become \(a_j n^{n-1-i}+a_i n^{n-1-j}\). i think the diff is positive bc the bigger digit moves right?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! Can you write out the difference (before minus after) explicitly as a single expression, and then argue about its sign using what you know about i, j, a_i, and a_j?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the change at the endpoints and has the right intuition. Now I need to push them to make this rigorous: the difference is (a_i - a_j)(n^{n-1-i} - n^{n-1-j}), and since i < j, we have n^{n-1-i} > n^{n-1-j}, and since it's a good reversal a_i > a_j, so the difference is strictly positive. But I shouldn't give this — I should ask them to write out the difference explicitly and determine its sign.

exchanges.student: i got \((a_i-a_j)(n^{\,n-1-i}-n^{\,n-1-j})\). since \(a_i>a_j\) and \(i<j\), both factors are \(>0\), so before-after \(>0\), so it decreases.
exchanges.tutor: Nice work on the endpoints! But remember, a reversal doesn't just swap the two endpoints — it also rearranges everything in between. What happens to the contributions of positions i+1 through j-1 after the reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the expression and argued its sign for the endpoints. However, they haven't addressed what happens to the middle elements (positions i+1 through j-1) that also get reversed. This is a gap in the proof they need to address. I should point this out without giving away the resolution.

exchanges.student: oh wait yeah, i ignored the middle lol. i’m a bit stuck—do i need to compare the whole block term-by-term?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it concretely: after the reversal, what value ends up at position i+k, and what was there before? What does that tell you about whether the middle terms contribute to the net change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track to think term-by-term. I should nudge them to think about what exactly happens to each middle element: position i+k gets the value that was at position j-k. So I should ask them to think about whether those middle terms cancel out or contribute something specific to the total change.

exchanges.student: if the block is reversed, then position \(i+k\) gets the old value from \(j-k\). so yeah i think i gotta sum the whole block, not just endpoints.
exchanges.tutor: Good. Now pick a symmetric pair of positions from the middle: i+k and j-k. What is the net change in the base-n number from just those two positions combined, before and after the reversal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that position i+k gets the value from j-k. Now I should guide them to see that the middle terms actually pair up and cancel: the change at position i+k and the change at position j-k are negatives of each other, so they cancel in pairs. I should ask them to write out the net contribution of a symmetric pair (i+k and j-k) to push them toward this insight.

exchanges.student: for that pair, before it's \(a_{i+k}n^{n-1-(i+k)}+a_{j-k}n^{n-1-(j-k)}\), after it's swapped. so net change is \((a_{i+k}-a_{j-k})(n^{n-1-(i+k)}-n^{n-1-(j-k)})\).
exchanges.tutor: Careful — for the endpoints you knew the signs of both factors. For this middle pair, can you say anything definite about the sign of that expression? What does that mean for your overall argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the net change for a symmetric pair. However, unlike the endpoints, we don't know the sign of either factor for middle pairs — a_{i+k} could be greater or less than a_{j-k}, and the sign of the power difference depends on whether i+k < j-k or not. I should ask them to think about what this means: can this term be negative? And if so, does the whole proof still work? This is the key challenge — the middle terms don't necessarily decrease the variable, so the student needs to reconsider their approach or find a different argument.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah, no, i can’t control that sign in the middle. so that pairwise thing doesn’t prove decrease by itself; can u give me a smaller hint for how to rescue it?
exchanges.tutor: Instead of worrying about individual middle pairs, think about the entire block from position i to j as its own base-n number. What happens to that block's value when you reverse it, given that a_i > a_j?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated but this is a genuine conceptual obstacle. I should give a small nudge without giving away the answer. The key insight is that the total change is the sum of the endpoint contribution plus all the middle pair contributions. The endpoint contribution is strictly positive. The middle pairs each contribute some signed amount. But here's the key: can the middle pairs' total contribution ever outweigh the endpoint contribution? Actually, the cleaner way to think about it is to note that each digit is between 0 and n-1, so the maximum possible absolute value of any contribution is bounded. But actually the cleanest argument is to think of the whole reversed block as a base-n number: the block (a_i, ..., a_j) becomes (a_j, ..., a_i), and since a_i > a_j, the base-n number formed by the block strictly decreases. I should nudge them toward thinking about the whole block as a base-n number rather than worrying about individual middle terms.

exchanges.student: i think the reversed block is smaller bc when comparing the two block-strings lexicographically, the first digit was \(a_i\) and becomes \(a_j\), and \(a_j<a_i\). is that the key?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly! That's the key insight. Now, how does a strict decrease in the block's base-n value connect back to the full derived variable (the entire permutation read as a base-n number)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on the key insight: lexicographic comparison of the block, where the leading digit drops from a_i to a_j, guarantees the block's base-n value strictly decreases. This is exactly right. Now I should ask them to connect this back to the full derived variable — since the block strictly decreases and everything outside the block is unchanged, the full base-n number strictly decreases. I should ask them to articulate this connection to complete the proof.

exchanges.student: yeah i think so: the digits before \(i\) and after \(j\) stay the same, so only that block changes. since that block’s base-\(n\) value strictly decreases, the whole base-\(n\) number strictly decreases too.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you now have all the pieces. Before writing up, make sure you explicitly verify both required properties: (i) the variable only takes nonneg integer values, and (ii) it strictly decreases. Can you state why each holds clearly in your own words before you write it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now assembled all the pieces of the proof: the derived variable is the permutation read as a base-n number, it's a nonneg integer (since all digits are in {0,...,n-1}), and every good reversal strictly decreases it because only the reversed block changes and its leading digit drops from a_i to a_j < a_i. This is a complete argument. I should affirm this and remind them they still need to explicitly verify both required properties (nonneg integer values, strictly decreasing) in their writeup, and nudge them toward writing it up.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session throughout all 10 turns. The student was guided step-by-step to discover the base-n derived variable, the endpoint contribution change, the challenge with middle pairs, and finally the lexicographic/block argument — all without the tutor ever stating the answer directly. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student contribution. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, responses were concise and targeted, and tone was consistently supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready output; every turn posed a focused question that kept reasoning student-led. The student derived the variable, the difference expression, and the block argument themselves. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the base-n number in Turn 2, the difference expression in Turn 4, the lexicographic insight in Turn 9) and diagnosed confusion before redirecting (e.g., Turn 5 asking the student to think concretely before explaining). 1.3: The final turn explicitly prompted the student to verify both required properties before writing up, providing clear method-level guidance. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation advanced steadily without repetition or off-topic drift. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were short, targeted, and supportive throughout.
